title: Lisa The Simpson

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer crashes car in opening credits
jokes.punchline: Classic Homer exclamation during accident
jokes.explanation: Iconic Homer catchphrase, perfectly integrated character moment with maximum economy

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Yuck. Salisbury soup again? My joe isn't sloppy enough. And talk about weak coffee.
jokes.punchline: Absurd complaints about already terrible food not being terrible enough
jokes.explanation: Solid observational humor about cafeteria food with good economy

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: The mashed potatoes are too mushy. I like mine crunchy.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse Van Houten
dialogue.line: And this mystery meat isn't mysterious enough. I can tell it's meat.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: The gravy should be gravier.

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: The Salisbury steak needs more... I don't know, Salisbury?

jokes.text: I bought my lunch at the gas station. It's the kind John Glenn eats when he's not in space.
jokes.punchline: Comparing gas station food to astronaut food when astronaut is on Earth
jokes.explanation: Clever absurdist logic with good economy and quotable phrasing

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Look at my lunch! I got it from the gas station. It's just like astronaut food!

dialogue.speaker: Astronaut
dialogue.line: I'm an astronaut.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you're on Earth right now.

jokes.text: Answer this, Professor. [Grunting]
jokes.setup: Lisa frustrated at not solving the puzzle
jokes.punchline: Destroys the puzzle package in anger
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's frustration, but predictable physical comedy

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Why won't you come together?!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Stupid puzzle!

jokes.text: Maybe I can't concentrate because of all your cooties! It's called lice. And it's nothing to be ashamed of.
jokes.punchline: Lisa matter-of-factly correcting the terminology and normalizing head lice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment - pedantic correction delivered with complete sincerity

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: I can't concentrate! It's cooties!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Actually, they're called head lice. And they're nothing to be ashamed of — they're a common parasitic infestation found in schools worldwide.

jokes.text: It's not Egyptian. It's not Prince's names.
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to solve the puzzle
jokes.punchline: References Prince's unpronounceable symbol period
jokes.explanation: Sharp cultural reference with excellent economy

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I can't solve this puzzle.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Have you tried the symbol from Prince's name?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's not a letter, Dad. It's unpronounceable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, it worked for him.

jokes.text: Look at my 'aggieculture' project. It's a piece of a cow.
jokes.punchline: Mispronounces 'agriculture' and shows literal cow piece
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective mispronunciation gag with absurd literal interpretation

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: For my agriculture project, I've brought in a real specimen.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: This here is a genuine piece of... ag-ri-cul-CHAR.

jokes.text: It's an eraser with pushpins. And a spring for his curly tail. [Laughs] Oink, oink, oink.
jokes.setup: Lisa desperately trying to pass off a hastily made project
jokes.punchline: Pathetic attempt to sell terrible craft project with pig sounds
jokes.explanation: Great cringe moment showing Lisa's uncharacteristic desperation

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Um, well, I made this out of macaroni and... okay, it's mostly just macaroni and glue, but if you look at it from this angle and squint really hard, it's a pig!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oink! Oink!

jokes.text: Oh, it looks like somebody's 'hoggir Dazs' ice cream. [Laughs] I should write this down.
jokes.setup: Apu looking at Lisa's pig project
jokes.punchline: Makes pig pun on Häagen-Dazs and is pleased with his own wit
jokes.explanation: Classic Apu moment - proud of his wordplay and wanting to document it

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Ah, what a delightful swine! You know, this reminds me of the Häagen-Dazs ice cream... or should I say, Häagen-Dazs pig! Ha! Oink-oink! Oh, I am very pleased with myself.

jokes.text: Oh, no! Her clothes are coming off! [Chuckles] Hey, you know who would love this? Men.
jokes.setup: Customer discovers novelty pen with revealing woman
jokes.punchline: Apu's matter-of-fact statement about male interest
jokes.explanation: Perfectly deadpan Apu delivery with excellent economy

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Wow, a pen where the clothes disappear when you turn it upside down!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Yes, we have had many satisfied customers. Men, women, children.

jokes.text: Thaw me out when robot wives are cheap and effective. P.S. Please alter my pants as fashion dictates.
jokes.setup: Note from frozen man explaining his plan
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous future expectations followed by mundane fashion concern
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from sci-fi concept to petty vanity concern - highly quotable

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: When I wake up in the year 3000, I expect to see flying cars, robot butlers, and cities on Mars.

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: But first, I need to know: will these pants still be in style?

jokes.text: Oh, no! No, no! The George Raft look is dead. I wanted Audie Murphy! If you would just apologize to Louie, you could get your hair cut the way you want.
jokes.punchline: Obscure 1940s actor references for hairstyles
jokes.explanation: Good character moment with obscure cultural references typical of Homer's random knowledge

jokes.text: I burned the roast. My cake fell. And I ran out of butter, so I had to spray the English muffins with PAM.
jokes.punchline: Escalates from cooking failures to absurd PAM spray substitute
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with relatable domestic absurdity

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, first I burned the roast, then I dropped the potatoes on the floor, and to top it off, I used PAM cooking spray... except it wasn't PAM, it was industrial-strength adhesive!

jokes.text: Maybe you're just a lousy cook. Ow!
jokes.punchline: Immediate physical retaliation from Marge
jokes.explanation: Basic setup/payoff but predictable physical comedy

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, Mom, you're really letting yourself go. Maybe you should hit the gym instead of hitting me all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Why you little!

jokes.text: Oh, ain't no big deal. All Simpsons start to lose their smarts around your age.
jokes.punchline: Grampa casually drops devastating family curse
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa delivery - drops bombshell casually while establishing episode premise

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm worried I'm getting dumber.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: You? Dumber? That's impossible. You're the smart one. Your father's the dumb one. Your mother's no genius. Your brother's an idiot. And don't get me started on your sister. But you? You've always been the smart Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Thanks, Grampa.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Of course, the Simpson men are all idiots. It's genetic. Runs in the family. My father was an idiot, I'm an idiot, your father's an idiot, and Bart's gonna be an idiot. It's a curse.

jokes.text: Oh, your dad used to be smart as a monkey. Then his mind started gettir lazy... and now he's dumb as a chimp.
jokes.punchline: Monkey to chimp progression (both primates but presented as decline)
jokes.explanation: Brilliant false hierarchy joke - monkeys and chimps are both primates but presented as intelligence scale

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Your father's brain is like a primate's - started as a monkey, now he's a chimp.

jokes.text: Back when he was your age, he was smart as a chimp.
jokes.punchline: Bart started even lower on the fake intelligence scale
jokes.explanation: Effective callback that extends the monkey/chimp hierarchy joke

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Bart started even lower on the fake intelligence scale

jokes.text: Wait. That's not how you spell 'dumbening.' Wait. 'Dumbening' isn't even a word!
jokes.setup: Lisa writing in her diary about intelligence decline
jokes.punchline: Realizes she's making up words, proving the decline is real
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-aware joke where Lisa's fear becomes reality in real time - perfect character integration

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dear diary, today I realized that the decline in intelligence is affecting even me. I used the word 'obfusticate' three times and I don't even know what it means.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait... I think I just made that word up. Oh my God, it's working. The dumbing down of society has claimed me.

jokes.text: Have you ever wondered why fat parents have fat children... or why Chinese parents have Chinese children?
jokes.punchline: Equates behavioral traits with ethnic identity as if they're the same type of inheritance
jokes.explanation: Classic Troy McClure absurdist logic presented with complete authority

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such educational films as 'Genetics: It's What Makes You You' and 'Why Do We Inherit Our Parents' Noses But Not Their Debts?'

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: Now, many people don't realize that all our behavioral traits are determined by our genes. For instance, Irish people are naturally drunk, Italians are inherently loud, and Jewish people are genetically cheap.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: It's all perfectly scientific!

jokes.text: You may remember me from such medical films as Alice Doesrt Live Anymore... and Mommy, What's Wrong with that Mars Face?
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous fake film titles with dark/absurd themes
jokes.explanation: Signature Troy McClure gag with particularly strong fake titles

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such films as 'Dial M for Murderousness' and 'Pukahontas'.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: And now, I'm pleased to present my latest work: 'The Erotic Adventures of Hercules' and 'Sgt. Bilko's Daring Great Escape'.

jokes.text: Isn't there any way I can change my D.N. A... like sitting on the microwave?
jokes.punchline: Suggests dangerous household appliance as gene therapy
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's scientific desperation

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Maybe I could use the microwave to alter my genetic code.

jokes.text: Hmm. He's got a lob... but I don't hear a dob. Oh. Uh, there it is.
jokes.punchline: Makes up medical terms that sound like nonsense
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dr. Nick character moment - fake medical competence with made-up terminology

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Nick
dialogue.line: Hmm, yes. Looks like a classic case of... acute frostibite-itis. I'm gonna need to thaw him out and apply some of my special medical cream. Now, let's see here... ah yes, the patient is experiencing severe hypothermia mixed with a touch of... freezeorrhea.

jokes.text: Hmm. He's got a lob... but I don't hear a dob. Oh. Uh, there it is.
jokes.punchline: Mispronunciation of 'lub-dub' heart sounds
jokes.explanation: Good Homer malapropism with medical terminology

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let me check if he's got a heartbeat.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lub-dub, lub-dub... No, wait. Lub-dub, lub-dub... Nope, he's dead.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Actually, I think I hear something. Lub-DUUUB, lub-DUUUB...

jokes.text: This freezer is an attractive nuisance. Just look at all that ice cream. It's damn near irresistible.
jokes.setup: Dr. Nick commenting on the freezer situation
jokes.punchline: Focuses on ice cream temptation rather than frozen human
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - uses legal term 'attractive nuisance' but applies it to ice cream not safety hazard

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Nick
dialogue.line: Well, the important thing is I've discovered that ice cream is a lot easier to resist than a human popsicle.

jokes.text: Come one, come all. See the amazing frozen man. Also gaze at the Frito found in a bag of Doritos.
jokes.punchline: Escalates from human spectacle to mundane snack food mix-up
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from extraordinary to completely mundane - perfect economy

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Step right up, step right up! Witness the most incredible, the most astounding, the most bewildering spectacles known to mankind!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: We have the two-headed man! The woman who can bend backwards! The boy who ate too much candy and got sick!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: But wait! There's more! Feast your eyes on the most horrifying sight of all...

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: A bag of mixed nuts where someone opened the almonds first!

jokes.text: Marvel at the floor that just won't come clean!
jokes.punchline: Presents cleaning failure as marvel
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the escalating mundane attractions

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Step right up, step right up! See the amazing self-cleaning oven! Watch as it cleans itself with the power of science!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Notice how the dirt and grime simply vanish! It's a miracle of modern technology!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: What's that? You say it's not working? Well, that's the marvel, my friend — it's so advanced, you can't even see it cleaning!

jokes.text: Marvel at the floor that just won't come clean!
jokes.punchline: Advertising basic store maintenance failure as attraction
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor turning mundane into spectacular

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Welcome, welcome to the Kwik-E-Mart Freak Show! Behold, the mop that cleans itself... by not cleaning anything! And here, our refrigerator that hasn't been defrosted since 1987!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, that's pretty gross.

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Gross? Homer, we charge five dollars admission. Last week, we made thirty dollars!

jokes.text: Well, Ralph seems happy enough. Maybe I should just give up now and settle into a mindless, happy stupor.
jokes.punchline: Considering embracing stupidity for happiness
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's intellectual struggle

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You know, watching Ralph be so happy all the time... maybe I've been thinking about this all wrong. Perhaps ignorance really is bliss. I could just... stop caring about things. Stop learning. Just embrace the simple life.

jokes.text: Man has always loved his buildings. But what happens when the buildings say, 'No more'?
jokes.punchline: Anthropomorphizes buildings as having agency in collapses
jokes.explanation: Absurd premise delivered with complete documentary seriousness

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: In a startling turn of events, the building simply decided it had had enough.

jokes.text: The best part was when the buildings fell down. Yeah. Ha. Maybe people got hurt. Hey, yeah.
jokes.setup: Father and son discussing disaster footage
jokes.punchline: Casual excitement about potential human casualties
jokes.explanation: Dark humor showing Homer and Bart's oblivious callousness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, look at this disaster footage!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Cool! What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't know, but there could be hundreds dead!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Sweet!

jokes.text: Show me your tie. It's cute. Come on. Let's go. Me and Frostillicus go back a long way.
jokes.setup: Ned protecting kids from inappropriate novelty
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual familiarity with frozen man
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast and Homer's casual naming of frozen man

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I'd better protect these children from that indecent novelty.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, Neddy! Frozen guy's a classic!

jokes.text: The label vanished many years ago. What strange wonders lie inside? Treasure, dog food? Whoa! Some things man is not meant to know.
jokes.punchline: Building mystery then backing down from revealing it
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and deflation with carnival showmanship

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: I have here the most mysterious can in all of Springfield. No label, no markings, completely unknown contents.

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: For years, it has sat on our shelf, defying identification. Today, I shall finally reveal what is inside...

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Actually, on second thought, I don't want to know. Some mysteries are better left unsolved.

jokes.text: Actually, it's not so much funny as it is long. It seems that there was this-
jokes.punchline: Self-aware admission that his stories are just long, not funny
jokes.explanation: Excellent self-aware Homer moment - knows his limitations but can't help himself

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, I got a funny story for you guys.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, actually, it's not funny. It's just... really long.

jokes.text: Marge, we need 5,000 c.c.'s of snacks, stat!
jokes.punchline: Uses medical terminology for snack emergency
jokes.explanation: Good Homer character moment treating snacks with medical urgency

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm experiencing acute crystalline fructose deficiency with a side of sodium chloride insufficiency! This is a medical emergency!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you just want chips and candy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't use big words with me, Marge. You know I don't understand them.

jokes.text: Marge, we need 5,000 c.c.'s of snacks, stat!
jokes.punchline: Using medical emergency terminology for food request
jokes.explanation: Good medical terminology misuse in Homer's dramatic style

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I need snacks, stat! This is a code red! Get me donuts, STAT!

jokes.text: If she started dinner three minutes earlier, we wouldn't be in this mess.
jokes.punchline: Blames Marge for not predicting their impatience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - shifting blame for his own impatience

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge! Why didn't you predict that we'd get impatient and eat all the snacks before the party?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you should have thought of it anyway!

jokes.text: But those candy bars are all squished and melted. Wha- Wha-
jokes.punchline: Homer and Bart's shocked silence at her criticism of their solution
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat showing how Homer and Bart are baffled by quality concerns

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Do you realize how much sugar is in that candy bar? It's basically just processed corn syrup and food coloring.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... candy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What's your point, sis?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: My point is that vending machines are engines of nutritional destruction. They should be removed from schools entirely.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Remove vending machines? But then where would I get my afternoon snacks?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and my chocolate bars are like, the only thing that gets me through math class.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You could eat an apple instead.

jokes.text: Hi, honey. It's me, your husband, Ralph. Hey, angel pie. Can you drive me down to the 'liberry'?
jokes.setup: Lisa imagining her future married life
jokes.punchline: Married to Ralph who mispronounces library
jokes.explanation: Great nightmare scenario for Lisa - perfect character choices for her fears

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I can't believe I'm married to Ralph.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Hey Lisa, I'm going to the lie-berry to return my book.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It's library, Ralph. Lie-brary. Why did I marry him?

jokes.text: Hey, angel pie. Can you drive me down to the 'liberry'? I wanna rent us up some movies.
jokes.setup: Lisa imagining future married to Ralph
jokes.punchline: Ralph's mispronunciation and grammar as adult
jokes.explanation: Perfect character consistency - Ralph never gets smarter

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: Hi Lisa. Me fail English? That's unpossible.

jokes.text: Wesley, get mama's pryir bar. Easy does it now. Easy. Little more. Get- Right- Back-
jokes.punchline: Needing crowbar assistance to get up
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy in Lisa's nightmare future

dialogue.speaker: Future Lisa
dialogue.line: A little help here!

dialogue.speaker: Future Homer
dialogue.line: Get the crowbar, Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Future Lisa
dialogue.line: A crowbar? I've only been sitting for twenty minutes!

jokes.text: By gum, it worked. I've awakened in the future. 'Moon Pie.' What a time to be alive.
jokes.punchline: Thinks Moon Pie represents futuristic advancement
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to freezing premise - mundane snack as symbol of future

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: Wha—? Where am I? How long was I frozen?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: About 200 years, old man.

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: 200 years?! What year is it?

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: My stars! What kind of advanced technology do you have in this futuristic age?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, we got this.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A Moon Pie.

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: A Moon Pie?! Extraordinary! What scientific marvels! They've finally made a pie... from the moon!

jokes.text: Is that you, Apu? Whoa! Time has ravaged your once-youthful looks.
jokes.setup: Old man recognizing Apu after 'time travel'
jokes.punchline: Commenting on aging when he's the one who was frozen
jokes.explanation: Good irony with time-displaced perspective

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: Apu? Is that you? You haven't aged a day!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Jasper, I've been frozen for twenty years.

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: Well you look terrible.

jokes.text: But the crowd kept looking at me like I was using too many augmented ninths.
jokes.punchline: Overly technical musical terminology for audience disconnect
jokes.explanation: Good musician character moment with believable pretentious terminology

dialogue.speaker: Jazz Musician
dialogue.line: Yeah, see, the problem is the audience just doesn't understand the chromatic modulations and the polyrhythmic time signatures I'm laying down. They're stuck in their pentatonic comfort zone, man. I'm playing in 7/8 with suspended diminished chords and they're still expecting a simple I-IV-V progression. That's why nobody gets it.

jokes.text: Uh, I am! I'm strongly opposed to... Proposition, uh, 305! You're against discount bus fares for war widows?
jokes.setup: Lisa desperately claiming to oppose random proposition
jokes.punchline: Accidentally opposes helping war widows
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff showing Lisa's desperation backfiring spectacularly

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm voting no on Proposition 2!

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: But Lisa, Proposition 2 provides aid to war widows and their children.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well... I still oppose it!

jokes.text: To Kill a Mockingbird, Harriet the Spy and Yertle the Turtle... possibly the best book ever written on the subject of turtle stacking.
jokes.punchline: Treats Dr. Seuss book with same reverence, absurd specific praise
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - treats children's book with academic seriousness and hyper-specific praise

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You know, everyone should read the great classics of literature. Take Dr. Seuss, for example. His use of anapestic tetrameter in 'Green Eggs and Ham' is simply masterful. The way he explores the existential dread of a creature forced to confront his own prejudices through the metaphor of colored eggs is nothing short of revolutionary.

jokes.text: Simpson gene? That's just foolishness! No. Baldness too.
jokes.punchline: Homer's matter-of-fact correction about baldness
jokes.explanation: Good quick reversal with Homer's deadpan factual addition

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, just because your father is bald doesn't mean you have to be.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, I AM bald.

jokes.text: Little girl... likes her brain. What's your opinion?
jokes.punchline: Reductive summary missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Good media reductionism humor

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: Intelligence isn't just about test scores or grades. It's about understanding complexity, questioning assumptions, and using knowledge to make the world better.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: In other news, local girl thinks she's smarter than everyone else.

jokes.text: Well, you look pretty successful. Thanks. I play a millionaire at parties. At least I'd like to.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to find success in family
jokes.punchline: Relative's 'job' is pretending to be rich, and he can't even get that work
jokes.explanation: Great escalation - from fake success to not even being able to fake success

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm trying to find success in this family.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, my job is pretending to be rich.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How's that going?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I can't even get that work.

jokes.text: Well, you look pretty successful. Thanks. I play a millionaire at parties. At least I'd like to.
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to find positive in relative's situation
jokes.punchline: Double disappointment - fake job he can't even get
jokes.explanation: Excellent double-deflation with escalating sadness

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, look on the bright side. At least you have a job prospect to work towards.

dialogue.speaker: Relative
dialogue.line: It's not even a real job, Lisa.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh... so it's a double disappointment - a fake job you can't even get.

jokes.text: I step in front of cars and sue the drivers. I beg celebrities for money. I'm a prison snitch. Jug band manager.
jokes.setup: Homer asking what relatives do for work
jokes.punchline: Increasingly pathetic and criminal occupations
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of pathetic Simpson relatives, with 'jug band manager' as perfect absurd finale

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So what do you guys do for work?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I'm retired.

dialogue.speaker: Patty
dialogue.line: We work at the DMV.

dialogue.speaker: Selma
dialogue.line: Yeah, crushing people's dreams one license photo at a time.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: I'm a helicopter pilot.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: I work at the nuclear plant with Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Same here.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: I run a bar.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Which is basically a waste disposal center for human beings.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What about you, Bart?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: School troublemaker.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm only eight.

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: Pacifier enthusiast.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And Marge?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'm a housewife.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So basically, we're all pretty much failures.

jokes.text: I beg celebrities for money. I'm a prison snitch. Jug band manager. My legs hurt.
jokes.punchline: Increasingly pathetic jobs ending with physical complaint
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with perfect anticlimatic ending

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm a Safety Inspector at the Nuclear Power Plant.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm a student.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm also a student.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I'm retired.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I guess that makes me the most successful one here.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well my back hurts.

jokes.text: Doctor? That's right. I'm Chief of Complicated Surgery at the InvasiCare Clinic.
jokes.punchline: 'InvasiCare' sounds medical but is clearly made up
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with fake medical facility name that sounds plausible

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: InvasiCare sounds medical but is clearly made up.

jokes.text: The defective Simpson gene is on the 'Y' chromosome, so only men are affected.
jokes.setup: Scientific explanation for Simpson family patterns
jokes.punchline: Actual genetics explanation that resolves the episode premise
jokes.explanation: Brilliant plot resolution that's both scientifically plausible and comedically satisfying

jokes.text: Unless you're a man. So wait a minute. This means I'm gonna be a failure? Yes, Son. A spectacular failure.
jokes.punchline: Homer's enthusiastic confirmation with upgrade to 'spectacular'
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor with Homer's pride in upgrading Bart's failure to 'spectacular'

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, you mean to tell me that I inherited my stupidity from you?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not just stupidity, boy — spectacular stupidity!

jokes.text: I got it! Whoo-hoo! I mean, splendid.
jokes.punchline: Catches herself celebrating like Homer, corrects to more refined expression
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Lisa catches herself acting like Homer and self-corrects

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Yes! I finally solved it! Woohoo!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: ...I mean, how delightfully gratifying.

tmdb_overview: Lisa struggles with solving a simple puzzle and fears that the "Simpson gene” is suddenly wiping out her intelligence. Homer stages a family reunion in hopes of introducing Lisa to some Simpsons she can respect.
insight: Lisa's intelligence crisis sustains a B-tier episode on character comedy alone, despite middling impact.
